question: List and briefly describe the three primary types of guided media used in computer networks.
expectedPoints: Twisted-pair cable: Consists of pairs of insulated copper wires twisted together to reduce electromagnetic interference. Common in telephone lines and LANs.
Coaxial cable: Has a single copper conductor at its center, surrounded by a plastic layer for insulation, a braided metal shield, and an outer cover. Used for cable TV and broadband internet.
Fiber-optic cable: Uses pulses of light to transmit data through thin strands of glass or plastic. Offers high bandwidth, immunity to EMI, and long-distance transmission.

question: Explain two key advantages that fiber-optic cable has over twisted-pair copper cable.
expectedPoints: Higher bandwidth: Fiber-optic supports significantly greater data rates and capacity over longer distances.
Immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI): Since it uses light, it is not affected by electrical noise, crosstalk, or external EMI, making it more reliable and secure.

question: Compare and contrast the characteristics, typical applications, and limitations of coaxial cable and unshielded twisted-pair (UTP) cable.
expectedPoints: Characteristics: Coaxial has a single central conductor with shielding, offering better noise resistance and higher bandwidth over longer distances than UTP. UTP is simpler, more flexible, and cheaper.
Applications: Coaxial is traditionally used for cable television (CATV) and broadband internet. UTP (e.g., Cat 5e/6) is the standard for Ethernet LANs and telephone wiring.

Limitations: Coaxial is bulkier, more expensive to install, and less flexible. UTP is highly susceptible to EMI and crosstalk, limiting its use in electrically noisy environments and over very long distances.
